cards.groundingNotes: In-show 'Rilke' quote confirmed; the line itself is a popular paraphrase attributed to Rilke, not a located verbatim quote (his real passage is the 'dragons as princesses' one). No primary page opened (WebFetch 403), grounding capped at 4.

cards.title: Ted's 'rom-communism' speech
cards.moment: With the team slumping, Ted announces he believes in 'communism' -- then, after a beat, 'rom-communism' -- his made-up word for a worldview lifted from romantic comedies: if everyone pulls together, it all works out in the end. He and the players reel off the genre's biggest stars.
cards.idea: A comic coinage that's really the show's whole creed in costume: hope as a deliberate choice. Like a rom-com, the promise isn't that nothing goes wrong, it's that you trust the happy ending and keep showing up for each other.
cards.why: This is the spine of the episode's romantic-comedy homage. Notice it's precursive faith -- belief offered before the evidence, dressed as a movie-genre joke so it lands light instead of preachy.
cards.source: Episode recaps and essays on the 'rom-communism' speech (PopMatters, PureWow, TV Insider, Looper).
cards.groundingNotes: Speech and star name-drops confirmed across multiple independent recaps; placement in S2E5 firm. No primary page opened (WebFetch 403), so grounding capped at 4.

cards.title: Roy takes Isaac back
cards.moment: Asked to fix a slumping team captain, Roy brings Isaac (and Ted) to a rough pitch under an underpass to watch a pickup game -- reminding him football is a kid's game you played because it was fun. Isaac's joy, and his form, come roaring back.
cards.idea: A callback to the show's recurring idea that joy, not pressure, is where good play lives. Roy diagnoses the problem as fear and overthinking, and the cure is simply remembering why you started.
cards.why: This is where Roy quietly discovers he can coach -- the seed of his whole next chapter, paid off later in the very same episode. Watch a man who's all gruffness turn out to be a natural teacher.
cards.source: Recaps of Roy's underpass intervention with Isaac and his revival, scored to Blur's 'Song 2' (ReadySteadyCut, TV Tropes, The Spool).
cards.groundingNotes: Underpass intervention, the 'football should be fun' message, and Isaac's turnaround confirmed across multiple recaps. No primary page opened (WebFetch 403), grounding capped at 4.

cards.title: Ted's panic attack
cards.moment: Mid-match, just as the game is turning Richmond's way, Ted is suddenly overwhelmed -- the noise drops away, his body feels wrong -- and he slips out unseen. Everyone assumes it is his stomach; Rebecca quietly recognizes it for what it is.
cards.idea: What looks like a stomach bug is a panic attack. The show insists this is not weakness or a flaw to hide -- it is something that happens to a strong, generous man, and the brave response is to stop pretending and let someone help.
cards.why: Handle this one tenderly. The point is not why it happened but that it happened to Ted of all people -- and that admitting you are not okay can itself be a kind of courage.
cards.source: Episode recaps (NPR, Nerds & Beyond, ShowSnob) and TV Tropes describing Ted's panic attack and exit during the match.
cards.groundingNotes: The on-screen panic attack and Ted's exit are confirmed across multiple recaps; the precise trigger is recappers' inference and is deliberately not asserted.

cards.title: Ted goes to Dr. Sharon
cards.moment: After the game, a shaken Ted is waiting in Dr. Sharon's darkened office. When she comes in, he tells her he is finally ready to make a real appointment.
cards.idea: The man who spends the whole show fixing everyone else chooses to be the one who needs someone. It quietly inverts his entire posture -- and opens the door to the season's real reckoning with mental health.
cards.why: This is the choice the panic attack sets up. Notice it is not a dramatic breakdown but a small, deliberate decision: asking, plainly, for help.
cards.source: Episode recaps (NPR, The Spool, Nerds & Beyond) of Ted seeking out Dr. Sharon and asking for an appointment.
cards.groundingNotes: Ted asking Dr. Sharon for an appointment confirmed across multiple recaps.

cards.tag: When a flaw is a tool
cards.title: Giving Jamie the signal
cards.moment: Roy decides Jamie has been coached into being too unselfish, and that the moment calls for his old ruthlessness. The agreed cue arrives mid-match: all four coaches flip Jamie off from the touchline. He promptly goads an opponent into a foul and buries the free kick.
cards.idea: A sly counter-melody to the show's redemption project. After weeks of teaching Jamie to be a team player, the staff deliberately switch the selfishness back on -- a small meditation on whether a flaw can be a tool, and whether 'be your best self' always means 'be nice.'
cards.why: Funny and a little subversive. The very trait they spent the season sanding down wins the match -- watch how the show refuses to make its lessons that tidy.
cards.source: Episode recaps (ReadySteadyCut, Rolling Stone, The Spool) and TV Tropes describing 'the signal' and Jamie's goal.
cards.groundingNotes: The middle-finger cue and Jamie drawing a foul to score confirmed across multiple recaps.

cards.title: Written by Brett Goldstein
cards.moment: Behind the scenes: this episode was written by Brett Goldstein -- the actor who plays Roy Kent. So the man behind Roy scripted the very hour in which Roy engineers 'the signal' and Ted quietly comes apart.
cards.idea: Goldstein started in the Ted Lasso writers' room before he was cast as Roy. In interviews he flagged that this is roughly where the season's gentle tone deliberately darkens -- a warning to anyone expecting nonstop sweetness.
cards.why: A good answer to 'why does the mood shift here?' The episode that turns serious was written by a cast member who knew exactly what was coming.
cards.source: Writing credit per Wikipedia, IMDb and the episode Fandom page; the tonal-shift framing per Goldstein's Gold Derby interview (paraphrased).
cards.groundingNotes: Goldstein's writing credit confirmed across listings; his interview comment about the darker tone is paraphrased, not quoted.

cards.title: Rebecca's match is Sam
cards.moment: After weeks of 'who is Bossgirl texting?', the show finally cuts to the other end of the chat -- and it is Sam, one of Rebecca's own players. The two of them still have no idea who they have been falling for.
cards.idea: A classic dramatic-irony reveal: we know, they don't. Two people have connected honestly and sweetly while completely blind to the complication staring at the audience.
cards.why: A genuine 'oh no' twist. Once you know, the earlier anonymous messages read completely differently on a rewatch.
cards.source: Episode recaps (TVLine, ScreenRant) and the Bantr Fandom page confirming the reveal that LDN152 is Sam.
cards.groundingNotes: Reveal that Rebecca's Bantr match is Sam confirmed across multiple recaps; spoiler scope is the season's romance arc.
